issues.description: Malicious obfuscated code detected. The file contains heavily obfuscated JavaScript that sets global variables, manipulates strings through character shuffling, and executes arbitrary code. This appears to be injected malware designed to execute malicious payloads while disguised as a configuration file.
issues.suggestion: IMMEDIATELY remove all code after line 9 (the closing brace and semicolon of the Jest configuration). The legitimate Jest config ends at line 9. Replace the entire file with only the clean configuration object. Investigate how this code was injected, scan all systems for compromise, rotate credentials, and review all files in the repository for similar injections.

issues.description: Global scope pollution detected with 'global['!']='10'' and other global variable assignments. The malicious code is modifying the global namespace to execute hidden functionality.
issues.suggestion: Remove all malicious code. This is part of an attack vector that could exfiltrate data, download additional payloads, or compromise the build/test pipeline.

issues.description: Use of 'eval'-like patterns through obfuscated function execution. The code uses string manipulation and dynamic function calls (dgC, jFD, xBg patterns) to hide its true intent and execute arbitrary code at runtime.
issues.suggestion: Delete all obfuscated code immediately. This technique is commonly used by malware to evade static analysis and execute remote code.

issues.description: String obfuscation using character code manipulation with patterns like '\x25', '\x23', String.fromCharCode(127). This is a clear indicator of malicious intent to hide payload content from code review.
issues.suggestion: Remove all code beyond the legitimate Jest configuration. Report this incident to your security team and consider this repository compromised.

issues.description: Immediately Invoked Function Expression (IIFE) with obfuscated logic at the end. The pattern '(function(){...})()' is executing malicious code immediately when this file is loaded by Node.js during Jest test runs.
issues.suggestion: Delete the entire IIFE and all associated obfuscation functions. Every test run is currently executing this malware. Stop all CI/CD pipelines using this code immediately.

issues.description: Supply chain attack detected. A legitimate Jest configuration file has been compromised with injected malicious code. This suggests the repository, developer machine, or dependency chain has been compromised.
issues.suggestion: Conduct immediate security audit: 1) Restore jest.config.js from a known-good commit, 2) Scan all repository files for similar injections, 3) Check git history for unauthorized commits, 4) Verify all developer machines and CI/CD systems, 5) Rotate all credentials and secrets, 6) Review package.json dependencies for compromised packages.

issues.description: The testMatch pattern could be more specific. Using '**/*.test.ts' will match test files anywhere, which may include node_modules if not properly excluded.
issues.suggestion: Consider using a more specific pattern like 'src/**/*.test.ts' or ensuring node_modules is excluded in Jest configuration to avoid unintended test discovery.

issues.description: Missing moduleFileExtensions configuration. While ts-jest preset provides defaults, explicitly defining file extensions can improve clarity.
issues.suggestion: Consider adding 'moduleFileExtensions: ['ts', 'tsx', 'js', 'jsx', 'json']' for explicit configuration.

codeQuality.summary: CRITICAL SECURITY BREACH: This file contains heavily obfuscated malicious code injected after a legitimate Jest configuration, indicating a supply chain attack or repository compromise requiring immediate incident response.

untestedPaths.reasoning: Jest configuration files are typically not unit tested as they are declarative configuration consumed by the Jest framework itself, not executable business logic
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: Validate that Jest can successfully load and parse the configuration without errors by running jest --showConfig

testCoverage.summary: Configuration file with no unit tests, which is appropriate as Jest configs are declarative and validated by the framework itself at runtime.

suggestions.description: Convert CommonJS module.exports to ES6 export default syntax for consistency with modern TypeScript/JavaScript practices
suggestions.benefits: Aligns with modern ES6+ module syntax, improves consistency across the codebase, and Jest supports ES6 exports in config files

suggestions.description: Add type safety by using TypeScript for the Jest config file (rename to jest.config.ts) and import Jest's Config type
suggestions.benefits: Adds type safety and IDE autocompletion for Jest configuration options, catches configuration errors at compile time, and improves maintainability

refactorings.summary: Jest config can be modernized with ES6 syntax, enhanced with TypeScript type safety, and improved with additional configuration options for better test coverage and execution.

issues.description: SQL injection vulnerability: The query method accepts raw SQL strings with parameters but provides no validation, sanitization, or parameterized query enforcement
issues.suggestion: Implement proper parameterized queries with prepared statements. Use a query builder library like TypeORM or Prisma, or validate and sanitize all SQL inputs. Never concatenate user input directly into SQL strings.

issues.description: Type safety violation: 'params' accepts 'any[]' allowing arbitrary data types to be passed unsafely
issues.suggestion: Replace 'any[]' with a more specific type like 'unknown[]' or create a union type of allowed parameter types (string | number | boolean | null)[]

issues.description: Return type 'any[]' provides no type safety for query results, making runtime errors likely
issues.suggestion: Use TypeScript generics to provide type-safe results: 'query<T>(sql: string, params?: unknown[]): Promise<T[]>' or define specific result types

issues.description: Private property 'data' is declared but never used in the implementation
issues.suggestion: Either implement the mock database to use this property for storing/retrieving mock data, or remove it if not needed

issues.description: Mock query method always returns empty array regardless of input, making it unsuitable for testing
issues.suggestion: Implement basic mock functionality that uses the 'data' Map to store and retrieve test data, or add a comment explaining this is intentionally non-functional

issues.description: Database instance exported as mutable constant allows external code to reassign it
issues.suggestion: Export as 'export const db: Database = new MockDatabase()' with type annotation, or make it a getter function to prevent reassignment

codeQuality.summary: Critical security vulnerabilities due to potential SQL injection and excessive use of 'any' types, combined with incomplete mock implementation and missing production-ready features significantly impact code quality.

untestedPaths.reasoning: Exported singleton is the public API used throughout the application; must verify it's properly initialized and typed
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: Test that exported db instance is a MockDatabase, implements Database interface, and can execute queries

untestedPaths.reasoning: Core database query method is currently a stub returning empty array; needs validation of params handling and return type
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: Test that query method accepts SQL string and optional params array, always returns empty array Promise, and handles missing params parameter

untestedPaths.reasoning: Default parameter handling needs verification to prevent runtime errors when params are not provided
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: Test query() called without params argument defaults to empty array and doesn't throw errors

untestedPaths.reasoning: Database interface promises async behavior; must verify thread-safety and proper Promise handling
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: Test multiple simultaneous query() calls resolve independently without race conditions or shared state corruption

testCoverage.summary: No tests found for src/database.ts; critical exported db instance, MockDatabase.query() method, and async/edge-case behavior are completely untested.

suggestions.description: Replace 'any[]' return type with a generic type parameter to improve type safety and enable better IntelliSense support for query results.
suggestions.benefits: Improved type safety, better IntelliSense, allows callers to specify expected result type, replaces 'any' with 'unknown' for safer parameter typing

suggestions.description: Remove unused 'data' property since the mock implementation returns an empty array and doesn't use stored data.
suggestions.benefits: Reduced memory footprint, clearer intent that this is a minimal mock, eliminates dead code

suggestions.description: Replace direct instantiation with a factory function or dependency injection to improve testability and allow runtime database switching.
suggestions.benefits: Enhanced testability through dependency injection, easier to swap implementations, supports configuration-based instantiation

refactorings.summary: The database module needs type safety improvements (replacing 'any' with generics and 'unknown'), removal of unused code (data property), and better dependency injection patterns for testability.

issues.description: Weak ID generation using Date.now() and Math.random() creates predictable IDs vulnerable to enumeration attacks and potential collisions in high-traffic scenarios
issues.suggestion: Use a cryptographically secure UUID library like 'crypto.randomUUID()' or 'uuid' package: import { randomUUID } from 'crypto'; return randomUUID();

issues.description: Custom HTML sanitization is incomplete and vulnerable to XSS attacks. Missing encoding for characters like '&', '/', and doesn't handle complex attack vectors (event handlers, javascript: protocol, etc.)
issues.suggestion: Use a proven sanitization library like DOMPurify or validator.js. For database storage, consider storing raw input and sanitizing only on output. Example: import validator from 'validator'; return validator.escape(input);

issues.description: db.query() return type is unvalidated and assumed to be an array. If the database returns a different structure or null, this will cause runtime errors
issues.suggestion: Add proper type validation and error handling: const result = await db.query(query); if (!result || !Array.isArray(result)) { throw new Error('Invalid database response'); } return result as Todo[];

issues.description: No validation that 'id' parameter is properly formatted before querying. Malformed IDs could cause database errors or unexpected behavior
issues.suggestion: Add input validation: if (!id || typeof id !== 'string' || id.trim().length === 0) { throw new Error('Invalid todo ID'); }

issues.description: updateTodoStatus doesn't verify if the todo exists before updating. Silent failures may occur, and no feedback is provided to the caller
issues.suggestion: Check affected rows and return status: const result = await db.query(query, [completed, id]); if (result.rowCount === 0) { throw new Error('Todo not found'); }

issues.description: deleteTodo doesn't verify if the todo exists before deletion. Deleting non-existent IDs succeeds silently, which may mask bugs
issues.suggestion: Check affected rows: const result = await db.query(query, [id]); if (result.rowCount === 0) { throw new Error('Todo not found'); } return result.rowCount > 0;

issues.description: getAllTodos retrieves all records without pagination. This will cause memory issues and slow response times as the dataset grows
issues.suggestion: Implement pagination: export async function getAllTodos(limit = 50, offset = 0): Promise<{ todos: Todo[], total: number }> { const query = 'SELECT * FROM todos ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT $1 OFFSET $2'; }

issues.description: Magic numbers (200, 1000) for length validation are hardcoded. Should be configurable constants for easier maintenance
issues.suggestion: Define constants: const MAX_TITLE_LENGTH = 200; const MAX_DESCRIPTION_LENGTH = 1000; Use these in validation

codeQuality.summary: The code demonstrates good intentions with parameterized queries and input validation, but suffers from critical security vulnerabilities in ID generation and sanitization, multiple high-severity bugs due to missing error handling and validation, and lacks pagination for scalability.

untestedPaths.reasoning: Core CRUD operation with database interaction and security sanitization is completely untested
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should create a todo with sanitized title and description, store it in database, and return todo object with generated id and createdAt timestamp

untestedPaths.reasoning: Error handling path when validation fails is untested, critical for API security
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should throw 'Invalid todo input' error when provided with empty title

untestedPaths.reasoning: Public API function for retrieving all todos is completely untested, including ordering logic
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should return all todos ordered by created_at in descending order (newest first)

untestedPaths.reasoning: Public API function for retrieving specific todo is completely untested
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should return todo object when given valid existing todo id

untestedPaths.reasoning: Error path when todo doesn't exist is untested, critical for API contract
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should return null when todo with given id does not exist

untestedPaths.reasoning: Public API function for updating todo completion status is completely untested
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should update todo completed status to true when called with completed=true

untestedPaths.reasoning: Public API function for deleting todos is completely untested, critical data mutation operation
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should delete todo from database when given valid todo id

untestedPaths.reasoning: Title with only whitespace characters should be rejected but current tests only check empty string, not strings that become empty after trim()
untestedPaths.suggestedTest: should return false when title contains only whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, newlines)

testCoverage.summary: Only 2 of 9 exported functions are tested (validateTodoInput and sanitizeInput), with all 5 critical CRUD operations (createTodo, getAllTodos, getTodoById, updateTodoStatus, deleteTodo) completely untested.

suggestions.description: Extract the sanitization logic for todo inputs into a dedicated function to reduce duplication and improve testability
suggestions.before:
const sanitizedTitle = sanitizeInput(input.title.trim());
const sanitizedDescription = sanitizeInput(input.description || '');
suggestions.benefits: Improved reusability, better separation of concerns, easier to test sanitization logic independently, reduces cognitive load in createTodo function

suggestions.location: createTodo, getAllTodos, getTodoById, updateTodoStatus, deleteTodo functions
suggestions.description: Extract database query execution into a repository layer to separate data access from business logic and improve testability
suggestions.benefits: Separation of concerns, improved testability with mock repositories, easier database migration, follows repository pattern, better maintainability

suggestions.description: Replace custom ID generation with crypto.randomUUID() for better uniqueness and standard compliance
suggestions.benefits: Better uniqueness guarantees, cryptographically secure, standard library support, prevents potential collisions in high-frequency scenarios

suggestions.description: Implement a service class pattern to encapsulate all todo operations and enable dependency injection for better testability
suggestions.before:
export async function createTodo(input: CreateTodoInput): Promise<Todo> { /* ... */ }
export async function getAllTodos(): Promise<Todo[]> { /* ... */ }
suggestions.benefits: Dependency injection support, easier mocking in tests, better encapsulation, follows SOLID principles, enables decorator pattern for logging/caching

refactorings.summary: The file would benefit from extracting concerns into separate layers (repository, service, validation), modernizing ID generation and null handling, implementing better error reporting in validation, and applying service/repository patterns for improved testability and maintainability.

recommendations.description: IMMEDIATE ACTION REQUIRED: jest.config.js contains obfuscated malicious code after line 9. This is a supply chain attack or repository compromise. Stop all CI/CD pipelines, remove the malicious code, audit all files, rotate credentials, and investigate the source of compromise.
ID generation in src/todo.ts uses weak Date.now() + Math.random() which is predictable and vulnerable to enumeration attacks. Replace with crypto.randomUUID() immediately.
Custom HTML sanitization in sanitizeInput() is incomplete and vulnerable to XSS attacks. Replace with a proven library like DOMPurify or validator.js to prevent script injection.

Database interface accepts raw SQL with no validation or enforcement of parameterized queries. While todo.ts uses parameterized queries correctly, the interface allows unsafe usage. Add type safety and consider using a query builder.
All 5 CRUD operations (createTodo, getAllTodos, getTodoById, updateTodoStatus, deleteTodo) have zero test coverage. Only helper functions are tested. This is a critical gap for production readiness.
CRUD functions don't validate inputs or check if operations succeeded. updateTodoStatus and deleteTodo don't verify if todos exist. getAllTodos and getTodoById don't validate database responses.
Excessive use of 'any' types in database.ts removes TypeScript's safety guarantees. Replace with generics for query results and 'unknown' for parameters.
getAllTodos retrieves all records without pagination, which will cause memory and performance issues as data grows. Implement limit/offset pagination.
Direct database calls in business logic make testing difficult. Extract database operations into a repository layer for better separation of concerns and testability.
Hardcoded validation limits (200, 1000) should be extracted as named constants for maintainability.
